_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
The ICAO Global Framework for SAF, LCAF and other Aviation Cleaner Energies, adopted at CAAF/3 in November 2023, imposes its primary compliance obligations on **aircraft operators** (airlines) and **fuel suppliers/producers** — not directly on freight forwarders. Under CORSIA (Annex 16, Volume IV), monitoring, reporting, and verification of CORSIA Eligible Fuel (CEF) claims attach to aeroplane operators for international flights between ICAO Member States. Fuel producers must be certified by an ICAO Council-approved Sustainability Certification Scheme (SCS), and only CORSIA-approved SCSs can certify fuel batches.

The workspace, as a freight forwarder, sits **upstream of the airline operator but downstream of the cargo owner** in the compliance chain — a position analogous to that of a 'facilitating economic operator' in the SAF value chain. The workspace does not itself hold CORSIA reporting obligations under Annex 16, Volume IV, nor does it carry fuel supplier obligations under ReFuelEU Aviation or the UK SAF Mandate (which attach to airport fuel suppliers). However, the workspace occupies a commercially critical node: it controls the choice of carrier and route, negotiates airfreight contracts, and consolidates cargo owner demand. This creates both an **exposure** (SAF surcharges and CORSIA costs pass through carrier tariffs) and an **opportunity** (the workspace can aggregate demand for SAF book-and-claim, negotiate SAF cost transparency clauses, and pass attributed environmental benefits to cargo owners for Scope 3 reporting).

Legal confirmation is required on two points: (a) whether any jurisdiction in which the workspace operates (EU, UK, US) imposes downstream SAF or carbon reporting duties on intermediaries acting as consolidators on international routes; (b) whether contractual arrangements with airline partners create any deemed 'economic operator' status under ICAO or national SAF schemes. The IATA SAF Registry explicitly recognises freight forwarder and intermediary roles and the workspace should seek qualified aviation-regulatory counsel in each key jurisdiction (EU/UK/US) before assuming it is fully exempt from direct obligations.

*Source: ICAO Sustainable Aviation Fuels page, ICAO, April 2026. https://www.icao.int/SAF; ICAO CORSIA Eligible Fuels page, ICAO, November 2025. https://www.icao.int/CORSIA/corsia-eligible-fuels; IATA SAF Registry, CADO/IATA, April 2026. https://airlines.iata.org/2026/04/14/ensuring-transparency-saf-environmental-benefits*
